back:
Use two pointers (left/right or slow/fast) to traverse array.

• Sorted array → opposite ends inward
• Pair/triplet sum → sort + two pointers
• Remove duplicates → slow/fast pointers

Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

Trigger: sorted array, pair finding, in-place ops

Maintain a window [left, right] that expands/shrinks.

• Fixed size → slide, update max/min
• Variable size → expand right, shrink left when invalid
• Track window state with hashmap/counter

Time: O(n) | Space: O(k)

Trigger: subarray/substring, contiguous, max/min length

Divide search space in half each step.

• Classic: find target in sorted array
• Boundary: find first/last occurrence
• On answer: search over result space

Always think: what condition splits the space?

Time: O(log n) | Space: O(1)

Trigger: sorted data, monotonic condition, min/max optimization

Stack that maintains increasing/decreasing order.

• Next Greater Element → decreasing stack
• Next Smaller Element → increasing stack
• Pop when current breaks monotonicity
• Process popped elements (they found their answer)

Time: O(n) | Space: O(n)

Trigger: next greater/smaller, spans, histogram problems

Efficiently track min/max in dynamic data.

• Top K → min-heap of size K
• Kth largest → min-heap, peek = answer
• Merge K sorted → min-heap of heads
• Two heaps (median) → max-heap + min-heap

Time: O(n log k) | Space: O(k)

Trigger: top/kth, merge sorted, running median

Key techniques for linked list problems:

• Slow/Fast pointers → cycle detection, middle node
• Dummy head → simplify edge cases
• Reverse in-place → prev, curr, next pointers
• Merge two lists → compare and link

Time: O(n) | Space: O(1)

Trigger: cycle, middle, reverse, merge operations

DFS vs BFS for tree problems:

• DFS (recursive): preorder, inorder, postorder
• BFS (queue): level-order traversal
• Return info UP → postorder (bottom-up)
• Pass info DOWN → preorder (top-down)

Height/depth → DFS | Level-based → BFS

Trigger: path sum, validate BST, serialize, LCA

Binary Search Tree invariant:
left < root < right

• Inorder traversal = sorted array
• Search/insert/delete → O(h)
• Validate → pass min/max range down
• Kth smallest → inorder + counter
• LCA → compare values, go left/right

Trigger: sorted tree ops, range queries, successor

BFS: shortest path (unweighted), level-by-level
DFS: explore all paths, connected components

• BFS → queue, visited set
• DFS → stack/recursion, visited set
• Grid → 4-directional neighbors
• Multi-source BFS → enqueue all sources first

Time: O(V+E) | Space: O(V)

Trigger: shortest path, connected components, islands

Order nodes so all edges point forward. DAG only.

1. Compute in-degree for all nodes
2. Enqueue nodes with in-degree 0
3. Process queue: remove node, reduce neighbors' in-degree
4. Enqueue neighbors reaching in-degree 0

Cycle detection: if result.length < numNodes → cycle

Trigger: prerequisites, build order, dependency resolution

Track connected components efficiently.

• find(x): return root with path compression
• union(x,y): merge sets by rank
• Path compression: point to root directly
• Union by rank: attach smaller tree under larger

Time: O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) amortized

Trigger: connected components, cycle in undirected graph, grouping

1. Define state: what info do we need?
2. Recurrence: how does state relate to subproblems?
3. Base case: smallest valid state
4. Order: compute bottom-up or memoize top-down
5. Answer: which state holds the result?

Optimization: often only need prev row → O(n) space

Trigger: optimal value, count ways, overlapping subproblems

• 1D DP: fibonacci, climbing stairs, house robber
  dp[i] depends on dp[i-1], dp[i-2]

• 2D DP: grid paths, LCS, edit distance
  dp[i][j] depends on neighbors

• Knapsack: include/exclude item
  dp[i][w] = max(skip, take)

• Interval DP: matrix chain, burst balloons
  dp[i][j] = best over split point k

Make locally optimal choice at each step.

• Sort by some criterion first
• Prove greedy choice is safe (exchange argument)
• Common: intervals → sort by end time
• Activity selection, jump game, task scheduler

Greedy ≠ always optimal. Verify with:
1. Greedy choice property
2. Optimal substructure

Trigger: scheduling, intervals, minimum cost

Always sort intervals first (by start or end).

• Merge: if curr.start ≤ prev.end → merge
• Insert: find position, merge overlapping
• Non-overlapping count: sort by end, greedy
• Overlap check: a.start < b.end && b.start < a.end

Time: O(n log n) for sort

Trigger: meeting rooms, merge intervals, schedule

Tree where each edge = character.

• Node: children[26], isEnd flag
• Insert: walk/create path, mark end
• Search: walk path, check isEnd
• StartsWith: walk path, check exists

Time: O(L) per operation (L = word length)
Space: O(N × L) for N words

Trigger: prefix search, autocomplete, word dictionary

Key techniques:

• Anagram check → char frequency counter
• Palindrome → two pointers from edges
• Substring search → sliding window + hashmap
• KMP → prefix function for O(n+m) matching

Common trick: sort chars or use freq[26] array

Trigger: anagrams, palindromes, pattern matching

Essential bit operations:

• n & (n-1) → clear lowest set bit
• n & (-n) → isolate lowest set bit
• XOR: a^a = 0, a^0 = a (find single number)
• Check bit i: n & (1 << i)
• Set bit i: n | (1 << i)
• Count bits: Brian Kernighan (n &= n-1)

Trigger: single number, power of 2, subsets via bitmask

Doubly linked list + HashMap.

• HashMap: key → node (O(1) lookup)
• DLL: maintains access order
• Get: move node to head, return val
• Put: add to head, evict tail if full

Time: O(1) for get/put
Space: O(capacity)

Also know: LFU cache (min-heap or freq buckets)

• BFS/DFS on grid: treat as graph, 4 directions
  dirs = [(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,0)]

• Spiral order: maintain top/bottom/left/right bounds
• Rotate 90°: transpose + reverse rows
• Search sorted matrix: start top-right corner

Time: O(m×n) traversal

Trigger: islands, shortest path in grid, word search

O(1) → hash lookup, array index
O(log n) → binary search
O(n) → single pass, two pointers
O(n log n) → sorting (merge/quick)
O(n²) → nested loops, brute force pairs
O(2ⁿ) → subsets, backtracking
O(n!) → permutations

Rule of thumb: ~10⁸ operations/sec
n ≤ 20 → 2ⁿ ok | n ≤ 10⁴ → n² ok | n ≤ 10⁶ → n log n

1. UNDERSTAND: restate problem, clarify edge cases
2. EXAMPLES: work through 2-3 examples by hand
3. BRUTE FORCE: describe naive solution + complexity
4. OPTIMIZE: identify bottleneck, apply pattern
5. CODE: write clean code, use helper functions
6. TEST: trace through example, check edge cases

Always communicate your thought process!

• "Sorted array" → Binary Search / Two Pointers
• "Subarray/substring" → Sliding Window
• "Top K / Kth" → Heap
• "Tree/graph traversal" → BFS/DFS
• "Count ways / optimize" → DP
• "All combinations" → Backtracking
• "Connected components" → Union Find / DFS
• "Prerequisites/order" → Topological Sort
• "Prefix lookup" → Trie
